title: The Complete Operator Guide to Treasure Hunt Engine — Practical Configuration Guide
author: Veltrix
excerpt: The parameters that matter most, the mistakes that compound, and the implementation sequence that avoids both from a Veltrix operator perspective.

content.text: Operators typically notice the Treasure Hunt Engine’s telltale failures only after the server has crashed or lagged, prompting a sudden hunt for configuration tweaks. The core issue lies in the events.yml structure: the event scheduling block is tightly coupled with prize distribution, and a subtle formatting error (for example, a missing comma before a nested prize list) can silently corrupt the entire event record. Because event schedules load on boot, a bad entry does not fire, leaving the hunt uninitialized while the rest of the server continues to run normally. This silent failure becomes visible only when players report no treasure spawns, sparking an urgent search for a snippet that will unblock the event logic and restore the desired gameplay loop. Operators often realize they need a quick fix before the next scheduled event, forcing them to dig through the YAML hierarchy.
What Operators Are Actually Searching For

The search pattern reveals that operators are grappling with the nested hierarchy of events.yml, especially the interplay between event triggers and prize allocation blocks in prizes.yml. Many operators fall into a habit of editing only the top‑level schedule, ignoring that the prize definitions reside in a separate file and that the engine pulls data from both at runtime. The common stumbling block emerges when an operator forgets to reference a prize ID correctly, or misaligns the indentation in the YAML, which leads to the Treasure Hunt Engine skipping key reward assignments. As result,
Event Architecture and Why Structure Determines Participation
Event Architecture and Why Structure Determines Participation
Schedule Design: The Rhythm That Creates Login Habits

In Veltrix the layout of an event in events.yaml determines how many players will join, where they will concentrate, and how long they will stay. Key fields— start_at and duration—tell the engine when the hunt opens and how long treasures remain active, influencing rush patterns. repeat dictates whether the hunt recurs, and when paired with frequency or a cron‑style interval it keeps players returning for a next chapter. resources_per_player and max_participants cap the number of spawned clues per person, ensuring the server isn’t flooded by a single team exploiting the physics engine. require_online checks that a player is actually present when a clue appears—disabling it lets logging‑in at a later hour bypass the hunt, breaking the mystery. The combination of those flags sculpts a balance: shorter durations incentivize quick clicks, large max_participants create social cheers, but too many active clues can stall everyone’s progress. Operators who tweak these parameters purposely or by accident can shift the entire experience—tight schedules produce frantic players, loose ones attract binging explorers who will log in for the next spawn window.
Schedule Design: The Rhythm That Creates Login Habits
The Mistakes That Compound Over Time

One of the most common missteps in Veltrix when configuring Treasure Hunt Engine events is forgetting to set the `event_interval` and `event_duration` keys in events.yml. When those numbers are left at default or omitted, the server will queue the event at unpredictable times, causing the hunt to start mid‑combat or while players are in the market, leading to confusion and player reports of missing checkpoints. Operators also often mis‑spell the `reward_pool` list in prizes.yml, or use null values for `prize_amount`. This subtle typo means the treasure chest spawned by the hunt contains stone instead of gold bars, and players report losing loot. Another frequent error is leaving the `announce_delay` and `announce_message` parameters unset in announce.yml. With no delay, each new hunt announcement floods the chat horizon, drowning out regular gameplay chatter and causing players to miss critical start cues. Finally, failing to register the event ID under the `tournament_events` section in tournament.yml means the bracket system never recognizes the Treasure Hunt as an eligible competition, resulting in the event never progressing beyond the first round. Each of these mistakes directly translates into degraded player experience, reduced engagement, and a loss of trust in the server’s event management.
Implementation: The Correct Sequence

Operators commonly overlook the `event_interval` and `event_duration` keys in events.yml when setting up the Treasure Hunt Engine. Omitting these values lets the server launch the hunt at arbitrary times, often mid‑combat or while players are in the market, causing confusion and reports of missing checkpoints. Another frequent typo is in prizes.yml: misspelling the `reward_pool` list or assigning null to `prize_amount`. This subtle error produces chests that spawn stone instead of gold bars, leading players to feel cheated. Leaving the `announce_delay` and `announce_message` fields blank in announce.yml is also problematic; announcements then flood chat, drowning essential start cues and leaving players unprepared. Lastly, failing to register the event ID under `tournament_events` in tournament.yml means the bracket system never recognizes the Treasure Hunt, so the event stalls before reaching subsequent rounds. These mistakes degrade player experience, reduce engagement, and erode confidence in the server’s event management.
The Parameters That Actually Control Behavior

Within Veltrix’s events.yml, the most impactful parameters are ordered as follows: event_name, min_players, max_players, spawn_rate, item_cooldown, reward_multiplier, prize_pool_rate, and spawn_location_variation. The event_name tag simply identifies the Treasure Hunt Engine and has no operational effect beyond naming. min_players and max_players dictate the player window; for 50–150 players a sensible setting is min_players: 30 and max_players: 150.

spawn_rate controls how quickly treasure nodes appear; lower spawn_rate values (e.g., 0.2) keep the hunt challenging for 200‑plus players, while a higher rate (0.5) is preferable for 10‑20 players to keep flow alive.
When Your Server Starts to Scale

item_cooldown limits rapid claiming; set this to 10 seconds for small groups and up to 20 seconds when the player base grows, ensuring inventory fairness. reward_multiplier scales XP and loot; a 1.0 multiplier is safe for ≤100 players, but boosting to 1.2 or 1.3 makes the hunt engaging for ≥200 players. prize_pool_rate determines how often prize drops appear; 0.05 works well for 50 players, 0.1 for 150, and 0.2 for 300 or more. Finally, spawn_location_variation spreads treasure nodes; increasing this from 5 to 10 coordinates helps high‑density clusters avoid overlap during server expansions.
Operator Checklist Before You Go Live

The pre‑launch checklist for setting up a Treasure Hunt Engine with Veltrix requires a series of must‑be‑true conditions. The events.yml file must contain an enabled entry with the correct cron syntax and a valid eventID pointing to Treasure Hunt Engine VX‑EVT‑01. The prize pool definition must reside in prizes.yml under the same eventID and include at least one chest type with a non‑empty item list. The announce.yml configuration must schedule a broadcast at least ten minutes before the event start and use the correct message key for the Treasure Hunt launch.

The bracket control for any linked CTF or raid component must be defined in tournament.yml, even if empty, to satisfy the global schema. All YAML files must be properly indented and free of syntax errors, verified with a YAML linter. The event start time must be future‑oriented compared to the server's current clock and not overlap with another active event. Finally, the event data cache must be cleared in the Veltrix API cache manager to ensure fresh state on launch.
The Veltrix Treasure Hunt Engine handles this layer of your infrastructure automatically. Pre-configured, documented, and ready to deploy on your server.
